clues: Evidence of this has been found in Egyptian mummies through physical signs of the disease as well as DNA in organs. It remains one of the top ten causes of death worldwide today
Also known as consumption historically due to the extreme weight loss it caused, concern about this disease played a role in the movement to prohibit public spitting

At the age of 13, Virginia married her first cousin, Edgar Allan Poe and at the age of 19, she began coughing up blood while singing at the piano. Unfortunately, she was only one of multiple women in Poe's life who died from this disease, which had a strong influence on much of his writing.
Developed in 1816 in Paris by Laennec, the market for this is now valued at over $630 million annually
While initially wooden tubes were used, rubber was introduced in the early 20th century, improving comfort and practical applications
Digital models of this device are now available that can amplify output up to 40 times more than traditional models

In the middle ages, grain contaminated with the Claviceps purpurea fungus caused catastrophic poisoning outbreaks. Victims suffered intense burning sensations, gangrene, and hallucinations. Scientists isolated this key causal alkaloid in 1918 which became a standard, highly effective treatment
With intense vasoconstrictive properties, this was first introduced as a migraine treatment based on the theory that migraines were caused by dilated blood vessels

This medication is frequently combined with caffeine in oral and rectal formulations such as Cafergot or Migergot to treat migraines because it dramatically improves the absorption in the digestive tract

In 2015, researchers recreated a 10th century recipe from 'Bald's Leechbook' consisting of a mixture of garlic, leek, wine, and oxgall (cow bile) brewed in a brass vessel for nine days. Surprisingly, this was shown to be remarkably adept at penetrating biofilms and eradicating about 90% of this bacteria
You probably have heard of Alexander Fleming's experiments where he found that a patch of mold was killing the bacteria around it, leading to the discovery of penicillin. What was that bacteria?
The name of this opportunistic bacteria comes from the Greek words meaning 'bunch of grapes' and 'berry' for the way cells cluster together.
Dionysius, tyrant of Heraclea (360-305BC) was affected by this disease and treated by physicians who would thrust fine needles through his ribs and belly whenever he fell asleep.
Up to 80-90% of cases may go undiagnosed, but this condition can increase risks of car accidents, hypertension, stroke, and heart failure

During the American Revolutionary War, military commanders strapped cannonballs to the backs of snoring soldiers as a field remedy for this disease to force them to sleep on their sides rather than their backs.
